_meta.project: B.Tech Mini Project — Virtual Chemistry Laboratory
_meta.keyFormat: IDs sorted alphabetically and joined with '+'. Example: HCl+NaOH (not NaOH+HCl)
_meta.howToAddChemical: 1. Add to DB.chemicals in script.js with id/name/formula/color/capColor/type. 2. Add to DB.shelfOrder array. 3. Add single-chemical entry in DB.reactions using the chemical ID as key. 4. Add all combination entries for new chemical paired with each existing chemical.
_meta.howToAddReaction: Key = both chemical IDs sorted alphabetically and joined by '+'. All required fields: title, formula, sub, badge, badgeColor, effect, liquidColor, bgColor, overview, chemNames, tools, safety, requiredSafety, steps, observation

HCl.title: 🧪 HCl — Strong Acid Dissociation
HCl.sub: Strong acid | Fully ionizes | pH < 1 | Pungent fumes
HCl.overview:
HCl is a strong acid that fully dissociates in water.
Releases H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions. Produces pungent fumes. Used in titrations and metal cleaning.
HCl.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Ensure fume hood is active before handling HCl.
Measure the required volume of dilute HCl (1M) carefully.
Transfer to a clean beaker using a glass funnel.
Observe: clear, colorless, pungent-smelling liquid.
Measure pH — should read < 2 on pH meter.
Test with blue litmus paper — turns red immediately.
Neutralize any waste with NaHCO₃ before disposal.
HCl.observation: Clear acidic solution. pH < 2. Pungent HCl fumes detected.

NaOH.title: 🧪 NaOH — Strong Base Dissolution
NaOH.sub: Strong base | Fully dissociates | pH > 13 | Exothermic
NaOH.overview:
NaOH dissolves in water with significant heat release.
Fully dissociates into Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions. Highly alkaline — pH > 13.
steps: Weigh out required amount of NaOH pellets.
Add pellets slowly to water — NEVER add water to NaOH.
Stir gently — significant heat is produced (exothermic).
Observe temperature rise on thermometer.
Measure pH — strongly alkaline (pH 13–14).
Test with red litmus paper — turns blue immediately.
Cool solution before using in further reactions.
NaOH.observation: Highly alkaline solution. pH > 13. Slippery feel. Heat released.

H2SO4.title: 🧪 H₂SO₄ — Strong Diprotic Acid
H2SO4.sub: Strong diprotic acid | Highly corrosive | ALWAYS add acid to water
H2SO4.overview:
H₂SO₄ is a strong diprotic acid that fully ionizes in water.
Dilution is highly exothermic — ALWAYS add acid to water, never reverse!
H2SO4.tools: • Beaker  • Measuring Cylinder  • Glass Rod  • Fume Hood
H2SO4.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield  • Fume Hood
steps: ⚠️ Always add acid to water — never add water to acid!
Fill a beaker with the required volume of distilled water first.
Slowly pour concentrated H₂SO₄ down the side of the beaker.
Stir constantly with a glass rod — enormous heat produced.
Allow to cool to room temperature before proceeding.
Measure pH — very low (pH 0–1).
Handle with extreme care — highly corrosive at all concentrations.
H2SO4.observation: Highly corrosive. Extreme heat on dilution. pH ≈ 0.

CuSO4.title: 🔵 CuSO₄ — Ionic Dissolution
CuSO4.sub: Ionic dissolution | Vivid blue Cu²⁺ solution | Weak acid | pH 3.5–4
CuSO4.overview:
CuSO₄ dissolves in water to give a vivid blue solution.
Cu²⁺ ions are responsible for the characteristic blue color.
steps: Weigh 5g of CuSO₄·5H₂O blue crystals (blue vitriol).
Add crystals to 100 mL of distilled water in a beaker.
Stir continuously until crystals fully dissolve.
Observe: vivid blue solution due to Cu²⁺ ions.
Measure pH — slightly acidic (pH 3.5–4).
Test conductivity — strong electrolyte.
Record: color intensity, clarity, and temperature change.
CuSO4.observation: Vivid blue CuSO₄ solution. Cu²⁺ ions give characteristic color.

NaHCO3.title: ⚪ NaHCO₃ — Thermal Decomposition
NaHCO3.sub: Thermal decomposition | CO₂ gas released on heating | Mild base | pH 8.3
NaHCO3.overview:
NaHCO₃ decomposes on heating to release CO₂ gas.
Also dissolves in water to form a mildly alkaline solution. Used in baking.
NaHCO3.tools: • Test Tube  • Burner  • Limewater  • Delivery Tube
steps: Place a spatula of NaHCO₃ powder in a dry test tube.
Gently heat the test tube over a Bunsen burner.
Observe: bubbles of CO₂ gas are evolved.
Pass evolved gas through limewater — turns milky (confirms CO₂).
Residue is Na₂CO₃ (sodium carbonate).
Record temperature at which decomposition begins.
Note: used in baking as a leavening agent.
NaHCO3.observation: CO₂ gas bubbles off on heating. Limewater turns milky.

CH3COOH.title: 🧪 CH₃COOH — Weak Acid Equilibrium
CH3COOH.sub: Weak acid | Partial dissociation | Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵ | Vinegar odor | pH ≈ 2.9
CH3COOH.overview:
CH₃COOH is a weak acid that only partially dissociates.
Equilibrium favors the undissociated form. pH ≈ 2.9 at 5%.
steps: Measure 10 mL of 5% acetic acid (vinegar strength) into a beaker.
Observe: clear, colorless, pungent smell of vinegar.
Measure pH — reads about 2.9 (weak acid).
Compare to HCl (same concentration): HCl is far more acidic.
Test with litmus — turns red (acidic).
Add NaOH drop by drop to observe neutralization.
Record equilibrium constant expression: Ka = [CH₃COO⁻][H⁺]/[CH₃COOH].
CH3COOH.observation: Clear acidic solution. pH ≈ 2.9. Distinct vinegar odor.

NH4OH.title: 🧪 NH₄OH — Weak Base
NH4OH.sub: Weak base | Partial ionization | pH ≈ 11 | Ammonia fumes
NH4OH.overview:
NH₄OH is a weak base with partial ionization in water.
Releases ammonia fumes — fume hood required.
NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Carry out all steps inside the fume hood.
Measure 10 mL of 10% NH₄OH solution into a beaker.
Observe: clear, colorless solution with pungent ammonia smell.
Measure pH — reads about 11 (weak base).
Test with red litmus paper — turns blue (alkaline).
Note: NH₃ gas is released — keep away from face.
Neutralize waste with dilute HCl before disposal.
NH4OH.observation: Clear alkaline solution. pH ≈ 11. Strong ammonia odor.

CaCO3.title: ⚪ CaCO₃ — Thermal Decomposition
CaCO3.sub: Thermal decomposition | Requires >840°C | CO₂ released | Lime production
CaCO3.overview:
CaCO₃ decomposes at high temperature (>840°C) into CaO and CO₂.
This is the basis of lime production in industry.
steps: Place a small piece of calcium carbonate (marble chip) in a crucible.
Heat strongly and continuously with a Bunsen burner.
Pass any evolved gas through limewater — confirms CO₂.
The white residue is CaO (quicklime).
Add a drop of water to the residue — hisses and heats up (CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂).
Test Ca(OH)₂ solution with litmus — turns blue (alkaline).
Record: temperature, time, and visual changes throughout.
CaCO3.observation: CO₂ gas evolved at high temp. White CaO (quicklime) remains.

Mg.title: 🔥 Mg — Combustion in Air
Mg.sub: Metal combustion | Blinding white flame | NEVER look directly | ΔH = −601 kJ/mol
Mg.overview:
Magnesium burns with an intensely bright white flame in air.
Produces magnesium oxide (MgO) as white powder.
Mg.tools: • Crucible Tongs  • Bunsen Burner  • Heatproof Mat
steps: ⚠️ NEVER look directly at burning magnesium — causes eye damage.
Hold a 5 cm strip of magnesium ribbon with crucible tongs.
Ignite the tip of the ribbon in the edge of a Bunsen flame.
Hold away from you — blinding white light is produced.
Allow to burn completely — white MgO powder falls.
Collect white powder on a heatproof mat.
Test MgO with water + indicator — forms Mg(OH)₂, alkaline.
Mg.observation: Intense white flash. MgO white powder produced. Alkaline residue.

C2H5OH.title: 🔥 Ethanol — Combustion
C2H5OH.sub: Organic alcohol | Flammable | Blue flame | ΔH = −1367 kJ/mol
C2H5OH.overview:
Ethanol is a flammable organic alcohol.
Burns with a nearly invisible blue flame. Also used as solvent and fuel.
C2H5OH.tools: • Evaporating Dish  • Crucible Tongs  • Burner  • Fume Hood
C2H5OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Ensure no open flames in vicinity before handling.
Pour a small amount (2 mL) of ethanol into an evaporating dish.
Place dish on a heat-resistant mat in the fume hood.
Use a lit splint to ignite the ethanol from a safe distance.
Observe blue, near-invisible flame — CO₂ and H₂O produced.
Allow to burn out completely or smother with a lid.
Record observations: flame color, heat produced, residue.
C2H5OH.observation: Blue nearly-invisible flame. CO₂ and H₂O produced. No residue.

HCl+NaOH.title: ⚗ Acid–Base Neutralization
HCl+NaOH.overview:
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O
Classic strong acid–base neutralization. Salt and water formed. Highly exothermic — temperature rises noticeably.
steps: Take a clean beaker and place it on the table.
Measure 10 mL of HCl (1M) using the measuring cylinder.
Carefully pour HCl into the beaker.
Add NaOH slowly, drop by drop, using the dropper.
Stir the mixture gently with the glass rod.
Observe — heat is released (exothermic reaction).
Test with universal indicator: pH should be ≈ 7 (neutral).
HCl+NaOH.observation: Solution heats up then becomes clear and neutral. pH = 7.

H2SO4+HCl.title: ⚠️ Strong Acid Mixture
H2SO4+HCl.formula: HCl + H₂SO₄ → H₃O⁺ + HSO₄⁻  (mixed acid, no net reaction)
H2SO4+HCl.sub: Mixed acid | No new compound | Highly corrosive | Fumes released
H2SO4+HCl.overview:
HCl and H₂SO₄ mix to form a highly corrosive acid solution.
No new compound forms — both acids coexist. Fumes released. Extremely dangerous.
H2SO4+HCl.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield  • Fume Hood
steps: CAUTION: Never mix concentrated acids carelessly.
Ensure fume hood is active and all PPE worn.
Always add acid to water, never water to acid.
Add H₂SO₄ slowly into the container first.
Add HCl dropwise. Observe fuming.
Keep away from face — corrosive fumes.
Neutralize any spills immediately with NaHCO₃.
H2SO4+HCl.observation: Fuming, highly corrosive mixed acid. Extreme caution required!

CuSO4+HCl.title: 🔄 Double Displacement — CuCl₂
CuSO4+HCl.sub: Double Displacement | Blue-green CuCl₂ | Solution becomes more acidic
CuSO4+HCl.overview:
CuSO₄ reacts with HCl forming copper chloride (blue-green) and sulfuric acid.
Solution becomes more acidic. Blue-green color indicates CuCl₂.
steps: Prepare CuSO₄ solution in a clean beaker.
Add dilute HCl slowly using a dropper.
Stir gently and observe color change.
Blue-green color indicates CuCl₂ formation.
Solution becomes more acidic (H₂SO₄ formed).
Test pH with indicator paper.
Record observations and color changes.
CuSO4+HCl.observation: Blue-green copper chloride (CuCl₂) solution forms. More acidic.

HCl+NaHCO3.title: 🫧 CO₂ Fizz — HCl + NaHCO₃
HCl+NaHCO3.sub: Acid + Carbonate | Vigorous CO₂ fizz | Endothermic | Classic fizz test
HCl+NaHCO3.overview:
HCl reacts vigorously with NaHCO₃ producing CO₂ gas.
Salt (NaCl) and water also formed. Used as classic CO₂ test with limewater.
steps: Place a spatula of NaHCO₃ in a beaker.
Add dilute HCl slowly using a dropper.
Observe: vigorous fizzing/effervescence — CO₂ gas released.
Pass evolved gas through limewater — turns milky white.
Reaction proceeds until one reagent is exhausted.
Final solution is neutral NaCl in water.
This reaction is used in baking (acid + baking soda).
HCl+NaHCO3.observation: Vigorous CO₂ fizzing. Limewater turns milky. Neutral NaCl remains.

CH3COOH+HCl.title: ⚗ Mixed Acid Solution
CH3COOH+HCl.formula: HCl + CH₃COOH → H⁺-dominant mixed acid solution
CH3COOH+HCl.sub: Acid mixing | No net reaction | HCl dominates pH | Pungent mixed odor
CH3COOH+HCl.overview:
HCl and CH₃COOH mix without reacting with each other.
HCl dominates pH. Solution is more acidic than acetic acid alone.
steps: Measure 5 mL of dilute HCl into a beaker.
Add 5 mL of acetic acid solution.
Observe: no visible reaction — both are acids.
Measure pH — lower than acetic acid alone (HCl dominates).
The pungent odor is a mix of HCl fumes and vinegar.
This mixture is NOT a buffer.
Neutralize with NaOH for disposal.
CH3COOH+HCl.observation: No visible reaction. Mixed acidic solution. HCl dominates pH.

HCl+NH4OH.title: ⚗ NH₄OH + HCl — Ammonium Chloride
HCl+NH4OH.sub: Weak Base–Strong Acid | Ammonium chloride (smelling salts) | pH ≈ 5
HCl+NH4OH.overview:
NH₄OH reacts with HCl forming ammonium chloride (smelling salts) and water.
Solution is slightly acidic at equivalence (pH ≈ 5.1).
HCl+NH4OH.tools: • Beaker  • Dropper  • pH Meter  • Fume Hood
HCl+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work in fume hood — NH₄OH releases ammonia.
Measure 10 mL NH₄OH into a beaker.
Add dilute HCl dropwise while stirring.
Ammonia smell decreases as HCl is added.
At equivalence: white NH₄Cl in solution.
Measure pH — slightly acidic (≈ 5.1).
NH₄Cl used in smelling salts and soldering flux.
HCl+NH4OH.observation: Ammonia odor reduces. NH₄Cl solution formed. pH ≈ 5.

CaCO3+HCl.title: 🫧 Marble Chips + HCl
CaCO3+HCl.sub: Acid + Carbonate | Vigorous CO₂ | Classic rate-of-reaction experiment
CaCO3+HCl.overview:
CaCO₃ (marble chips) reacts with HCl to release CO₂ gas.
Classic experiment for studying reaction rates — compare chips vs powder.
CaCO3+HCl.tools: • Conical Flask  • Measuring Cylinder  • Limewater  • Delivery Tube
steps: Place 2g of marble chips (CaCO₃) in a conical flask.
Measure 20 mL of dilute HCl (2M) in a measuring cylinder.
Pour HCl into the flask — fizzing starts immediately.
Connect delivery tube to limewater — turns milky (CO₂ confirmed).
Observe: marble chips gradually dissolve.
Reaction slows as CaCO₃ is consumed.
Repeat with powder vs chips to compare reaction rates.
CaCO3+HCl.observation: Vigorous CO₂ fizzing. Marble chips dissolve in acid. Limewater turns milky.

HCl+Mg.title: ⚡ Metal + Acid — H₂ Gas
HCl+Mg.sub: Metal-Acid Reaction | Vigorous H₂ evolution | Squeaky pop test | Exothermic
HCl+Mg.overview:
Magnesium reacts vigorously with HCl releasing hydrogen gas.
MgCl₂ solution remains. Squeaky pop test confirms H₂.
HCl+Mg.tools: • Test Tube  • Bunsen Burner  • Lighted Splint  • Tongs
steps: Place a small strip of magnesium ribbon in a test tube.
Add dilute HCl (2M) — vigorous fizzing immediately.
Observe: bubbles of H₂ gas rising rapidly.
Collect H₂ gas by inverted test tube.
Apply a lighted splint to the mouth of the tube.
Hear the characteristic 'squeaky pop' — confirms H₂.
Mg dissolves completely — MgCl₂ solution remains.
HCl+Mg.observation: Vigorous H₂ bubbling. Squeaky pop confirms H₂. Mg dissolves.

C2H5OH+HCl.title: ⚗ Ethanol + HCl — Chloroethane Formation
C2H5OH+HCl.formula: C₂H₅OH + HCl → C₂H₅Cl + H₂O  (requires ZnCl₂ catalyst)
C2H5OH+HCl.sub: Substitution | Chloroethane gas | Requires catalyst | Organic chemistry
C2H5OH+HCl.overview:
Ethanol reacts with HCl in the presence of ZnCl₂ catalyst to form chloroethane.
Gaseous product — pungent sweet odor. Requires heating.
C2H5OH+HCl.tools: • Round Flask  • Reflux Condenser  • ZnCl₂ catalyst  • Fume Hood
C2H5OH+HCl.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Mix ethanol and concentrated HCl in a flask.
Add anhydrous ZnCl₂ as catalyst.
Heat gently in fume hood.
Collect gaseous C₂H₅Cl (b.p. 12°C) — sweet pungent odor.
Do NOT inhale — chloroethane is toxic.
Wash any residue with water.
Dispose carefully as chemical waste.
C2H5OH+HCl.observation: Sweet pungent odor of chloroethane. Requires catalyst and heating.

H2SO4+NaOH.title: ⚗ Neutralization — H₂SO₄ + NaOH
H2SO4+NaOH.sub: Acid–Base Neutralization | Sodium sulfate salt | Strongly Exothermic
H2SO4+NaOH.overview:
H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH → Na₂SO₄ + 2H₂O
Sodium sulfate (Glauber's salt) formed. Strongly exothermic — use caution.
H2SO4+NaOH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield
steps: Measure 10 mL of dilute H₂SO₄ carefully into a beaker.
Prepare NaOH solution (2M) in a separate container.
Add NaOH slowly and dropwise to the acid — not the reverse.
Stir constantly — strong heat is produced.
Monitor temperature with a thermometer.
Test with litmus until neutral (pH = 7).
Na₂SO₄ (Glauber's salt) remains on evaporation.
H2SO4+NaOH.observation: Significant heat released. Sodium sulfate Na₂SO₄ in solution. pH → 7.

CuSO4+H2SO4.title: 🔵 Acidified Copper Sulfate
CuSO4+H2SO4.sub: Acidified solution | Prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation | Used in electroplating
CuSO4+H2SO4.overview:
H₂SO₄ acidifies CuSO₄ solution forming a stable electrolyte.
The acid prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation. Standard electrolyte for copper electroplating.
CuSO4+H2SO4.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield
steps: Prepare CuSO₄ solution in a beaker.
Add dilute H₂SO₄ slowly and stir.
Observe — solution remains bright blue.
The acid prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation.
This is the standard electrolyte for Cu electroplating.
Measure pH — should be acidic (pH 2–4).
Use in electroplating or Daniell cell experiment.
CuSO4+H2SO4.observation: Bright blue acidified CuSO₄ solution. Stable for electroplating.

H2SO4+NaHCO3.title: 🫧 H₂SO₄ + NaHCO₃ — Fire Extinguisher Reaction
H2SO4+NaHCO3.sub: Acid + Carbonate | Very vigorous CO₂ | Fire extinguisher principle
H2SO4+NaHCO3.overview:
H₂SO₄ reacts vigorously with NaHCO₃ releasing CO₂ gas rapidly.
This is the principle behind soda–acid fire extinguishers.
H2SO4+NaHCO3.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield
steps: Add NaHCO₃ powder to a beaker.
Add dilute H₂SO₄ carefully and slowly.
Vigorous fizzing — CO₂ evolved rapidly.
Pass gas through limewater to confirm CO₂.
Heat NOT required — reaction is spontaneous.
This is the principle of soda–acid fire extinguisher.
Neutralize residue with water before disposal.
H2SO4+NaHCO3.observation: Very vigorous CO₂ fizz. Na₂SO₄ solution remains. Limewater turns milky.

CH3COOH+H2SO4.title: ⚗ Mixed Acid — CH₃COOH + H₂SO₄
CH3COOH+H2SO4.formula: CH₃COOH + H₂SO₄ → H₃O⁺ + CH₃COO⁻ + HSO₄⁻  (mixed acid solution)
CH3COOH+H2SO4.sub: Mixed acid | H₂SO₄ acts as dehydrating agent | No net reaction | Corrosive
CH3COOH+H2SO4.overview:
H₂SO₄ and CH₃COOH coexist as a mixed acid solution.
H₂SO₄ can act as a dehydrating agent for acetic acid at high concentration. Corrosive.
CH3COOH+H2SO4.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield  • Fume Hood
steps: Work entirely in the fume hood.
Measure small volumes of each acid.
Mix carefully — no vigorous reaction at room temperature.
Observe: pungent mixed acid fumes.
Measure pH — very acidic.
H₂SO₄ can dehydrate acetic acid at high concentrations.
Neutralize all waste carefully with NaHCO₃.
CH3COOH+H2SO4.observation: Highly corrosive mixed acid. Pungent fumes. Very low pH.

H2SO4+NH4OH.title: ⚗ NH₄OH + H₂SO₄ — Ammonium Sulfate Fertilizer
H2SO4+NH4OH.sub: Weak Base–Strong Acid | Ammonium sulfate fertilizer | pH ≈ 5
H2SO4+NH4OH.overview:
NH₄OH + H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄ + H₂O
Ammonium sulfate — an important nitrogen fertilizer — is formed.
H2SO4+NH4OH.tools: • Beaker  • Dropper  • Fume Hood  • Evaporating Dish
H2SO4+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield  • Fume Hood
steps: Work in fume hood — NH₃ fumes from NH₄OH.
Add NH₄OH solution to a beaker.
Add dilute H₂SO₄ slowly and carefully.
Stir continuously — ammonia smell reduces.
At equivalence: colorless (NH₄)₂SO₄ solution.
Evaporate gently to obtain white crystalline (NH₄)₂SO₄.
Used commercially as nitrogen fertilizer.
H2SO4+NH4OH.observation: Colorless (NH₄)₂SO₄ solution. Ammonia smell gone. White crystals on evaporation.

CaCO3+H2SO4.title: 🫧 CaCO₃ + H₂SO₄ — Self-Limiting Reaction
CaCO3+H2SO4.sub: Self-limiting | Insoluble CaSO₄ crust stops reaction | Brief CO₂ then stops
CaCO3+H2SO4.overview:
CaCO₃ reacts with H₂SO₄ but quickly forms an insoluble CaSO₄ crust.
This crust stops further reaction — H₂SO₄ is unsuitable for gas preparation from carbonates.
CaCO3+H2SO4.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield
steps: Place marble chips into a beaker.
Add dilute H₂SO₄ carefully.
Initial fizzing observed — CO₂ released briefly.
Fizzing stops quickly — insoluble CaSO₄ coats the marble.
This 'passivation' prevents further reaction.
Compare with HCl — HCl reacts continuously, H₂SO₄ stops.
This demonstrates why dilute H₂SO₄ is unsuitable for gas preparation.
CaCO3+H2SO4.observation: Brief fizzing then stops. White CaSO₄ crust coats marble — self-limiting.

H2SO4+Mg.title: ⚡ Magnesium + H₂SO₄ — Epsom Salt
H2SO4+Mg.sub: Metal-Acid Reaction | H₂ gas evolved | MgSO₄ (Epsom salt) formed
H2SO4+Mg.overview:
Mg reacts with dilute H₂SO₄ releasing H₂ gas.
Magnesium sulfate (Epsom salt, used in bath salts) remains in solution.
H2SO4+Mg.tools: • Test Tube  • Lighted Splint  • Measuring Cylinder
H2SO4+Mg.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield
steps: Place magnesium ribbon in a test tube.
Add dilute H₂SO₄ carefully.
Observe vigorous effervescence — H₂ gas evolved.
Test evolved gas with burning splint — squeaky pop.
Solution turns colorless — MgSO₄ (Epsom salt) forms.
Note: concentrated H₂SO₄ may produce SO₂ instead of H₂.
Dispose of MgSO₄ solution safely.
H2SO4+Mg.observation: Vigorous H₂ fizzing. Clear MgSO₄ (Epsom salt) solution forms.

C2H5OH+H2SO4.title: 🧴 Ethanol + H₂SO₄ — Dehydration / Esterification Catalyst
C2H5OH+H2SO4.formula: C₂H₅OH + H₂SO₄ → C₂H₄ + H₂O  (170°C)  OR  H₂SO₄ acts as esterification catalyst
C2H5OH+H2SO4.sub: Dehydration at 170°C → Ethylene | Catalyst at 60°C for ester synthesis
C2H5OH+H2SO4.overview:
At 170°C: H₂SO₄ dehydrates ethanol to produce ethylene gas.
At ~60°C: H₂SO₄ acts as catalyst for esterification. Both require fume hood.
C2H5OH+H2SO4.tools: • Round Flask  • Thermometer  • Delivery Tube  • Fume Hood
C2H5OH+H2SO4.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Face Shield  • Fume Hood
steps: Carry out entirely in fume hood.
Mix ethanol and conc. H₂SO₄ carefully (add acid to ethanol).
For dehydration: heat to 170°C — ethylene gas evolved.
For esterification catalyst: use at ~60°C with a carboxylic acid.
Observe: charring may occur (H₂SO₄ dehydrates organics).
Wash all glassware immediately after use.
Neutralize waste with NaHCO₃.
C2H5OH+H2SO4.observation: Ethylene gas (dehydration at 170°C) or ester aroma (catalyst mode).

CuSO4+NaOH.title: 🔵 Precipitation — Cu(OH)₂
CuSO4+NaOH.sub: Double Displacement | Pale blue Cu(OH)₂ precipitate | Classic Cu²⁺ test
CuSO4+NaOH.overview:
CuSO₄ + 2NaOH → Cu(OH)₂↓ + Na₂SO₄
Pale blue gelatinous precipitate of Cu(OH)₂ forms. Classic qualitative test for Cu²⁺ ions.
steps: Take a clean test tube.
Add 5 mL of CuSO₄ (blue) solution.
Add NaOH solution slowly, drop by drop.
Observe: pale blue gelatinous precipitate — Cu(OH)₂.
Allow precipitate to settle at the bottom.
Heat the tube gently — precipitate turns black (CuO forms).
Record: initial color, precipitate color, and change on heating.
CuSO4+NaOH.observation: Pale blue Cu(OH)₂ precipitate. Turns black CuO on heating.

CuSO4+NaHCO3.title: 🔵 CuSO₄ + NaHCO₃ — Malachite (Basic Copper Carbonate)
CuSO4+NaHCO3.sub: Double Displacement | Green-blue malachite precipitate | CO₂ also evolved
CuSO4+NaHCO3.overview:
CuSO₄ + NaHCO₃ forms basic copper carbonate (malachite — green mineral).
CO₂ gas is also evolved. The green precipitate is the mineral malachite.
steps: Add 5 mL of CuSO₄ solution to a test tube.
Add NaHCO₃ solution dropwise.
Observe: pale green-blue precipitate forms immediately.
CO₂ gas also evolved — fizzing visible.
Precipitate is basic copper carbonate Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃ (malachite).
Collect precipitate by filtration.
This is the natural mineral malachite.
CuSO4+NaHCO3.observation: Green-blue malachite precipitate (Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃). CO₂ gas evolved.

CH3COOH+CuSO4.title: 🔵 CuSO₄ + CH₃COOH — Copper Acetate
CH3COOH+CuSO4.sub: Salt metathesis | Copper acetate in solution | Slow exchange reaction
CH3COOH+CuSO4.overview:
CuSO₄ and CH₃COOH can form copper acetate in solution.
Reaction is slow and partial. Copper acetate has uses in dye manufacturing.
steps: Dissolve CuSO₄ in water in a beaker.
Add glacial acetic acid or concentrated CH₃COOH.
Heat gently with stirring.
Blue-green color maintained — Cu(CH₃COO)₂ forms.
Solution remains acidic due to H₂SO₄ also produced.
Cool and observe crystal formation on evaporation.
Copper acetate used in dyes and wood preservatives.
CH3COOH+CuSO4.observation: Blue-green copper acetate solution. Slow exchange at room temperature.

CuSO4+NH4OH.title: 🔷 Deep Blue Tetraammine Copper Complex
CuSO4+NH4OH.sub: Complex ion formation | Deep royal blue [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ | Striking color change
CuSO4+NH4OH.overview:
Excess NH₄OH reacts with CuSO₄ to form the intensely deep blue tetraammincopper(II) complex.
Dramatic color change from pale blue to deep royal blue.
CuSO4+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Add 5 mL of CuSO₄ solution to a test tube.
Add a few drops of NH₄OH — pale blue precipitate forms first.
Continue adding excess NH₄OH — precipitate dissolves.
Observe: dramatic change to intensely deep royal blue color.
This is the [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ tetraammine complex ion.
Used as a qualitative test for Cu²⁺ ions.
Work in fume hood — ammonia fumes are released.
CuSO4+NH4OH.observation: Dramatic pale blue → deep royal blue. [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ complex formed.

CaCO3+CuSO4.title: 🔄 CaCO₃ + CuSO₄ — Double Displacement
CaCO3+CuSO4.formula: CaCO₃ + CuSO₄ → CuCO₃↓ + CaSO₄↓  (both insoluble, slow)
CaCO3+CuSO4.sub: Double Displacement | Two precipitates | Very slow reaction in dilute solution
CaCO3+CuSO4.overview:
CaCO₃ and CuSO₄ can theoretically undergo double displacement.
Both products (CuCO₃ and CaSO₄) are insoluble — reaction is very slow in dilute solution.
steps: Add CuSO₄ solution to a beaker.
Add powdered CaCO₃ and stir.
Reaction is very slow at room temperature.
Heat may accelerate slightly.
Green-blue CuCO₃ and white CaSO₄ may form on evaporation.
Filter to collect precipitates.
This reaction demonstrates the common-ion effect.
CaCO3+CuSO4.observation: Very slow reaction. Green-blue CuCO₃ and white CaSO₄ precipitate on evaporation.

CuSO4+Mg.title: ⚡ Single Displacement — Copper Deposition
CuSO4+Mg.sub: Single Displacement | Copper metal deposits | Mg displaces Cu | Exothermic
CuSO4+Mg.overview:
Magnesium (more reactive) displaces copper from CuSO₄ solution.
Red-brown copper metal deposits on the Mg surface. Solution becomes colorless (MgSO₄).
steps: Pour CuSO₄ solution into a clean beaker.
Place a strip of Mg ribbon into the blue solution.
Observe immediately: red-brown copper deposits on Mg surface.
The blue color of the solution fades as Cu²⁺ is used up.
MgSO₄ (colorless) forms in solution.
Remove the Mg strip — coated with red-brown copper metal.
This demonstrates the reactivity series — Mg > Cu.
CuSO4+Mg.observation: Red-brown Cu metal deposits on Mg. Blue color fades — Mg is above Cu in reactivity series.

C2H5OH+CuSO4.title: 🔵 Ethanol + CuSO₄ — No Significant Reaction
C2H5OH+CuSO4.formula: C₂H₅OH + CuSO₄ → No significant reaction at room temperature
C2H5OH+CuSO4.sub: No net reaction | Cu²⁺ may weakly coordinate with ethanol | Miscible liquids
C2H5OH+CuSO4.overview:
Ethanol and aqueous CuSO₄ do not react significantly at room temperature.
Cu²⁺ ions remain in solution. The mixture remains blue.
steps: Add CuSO₄ solution to a beaker.
Add ethanol and stir.
No visible reaction — solution remains blue.
No precipitate, no gas, no color change.
CuSO₄ can be used to detect water in ethanol (turns white anhydrous CuSO₄ blue).
Record: color, clarity, and any temperature change.
No significant chemical reaction has occurred.
C2H5OH+CuSO4.observation: No reaction at room temperature. Blue solution maintained.

NaHCO3+NaOH.title: 🧪 NaHCO₃ + NaOH — Sodium Carbonate Formation
NaHCO3+NaOH.sub: Base + Weak base salt | Na₂CO₃ (washing soda) formed | Mildly exothermic
NaHCO3+NaOH.overview:
NaHCO₃ reacts with NaOH to form sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃ — washing soda).
Solution becomes strongly alkaline.
steps: Dissolve NaHCO₃ in water in a beaker.
Add NaOH solution and stir.
Observe: no visible reaction — solutions mix.
Measure pH — strongly alkaline (pH > 11).
Na₂CO₃ (sodium carbonate / washing soda) forms.
Evaporate gently to recover Na₂CO₃ crystals.
This is used industrially to produce washing soda.
NaHCO3+NaOH.observation: Strongly alkaline Na₂CO₃ solution formed. pH > 11.

CH3COOH+NaOH.title: ⚗ Acetic Acid + NaOH — Titration
CH3COOH+NaOH.sub: Weak Acid–Strong Base | Sodium acetate | Buffer region | pH > 7 at equivalence
CH3COOH+NaOH.overview:
CH₃COOH + NaOH → CH₃COONa + H₂O
Sodium acetate (a salt) formed. Slightly alkaline at equivalence point (pH ≈ 8.7). Shows buffer behavior.
CH3COOH+NaOH.tools: • Beaker  • Burette  • pH Meter  • Phenolphthalein Indicator
steps: Add 10 mL of acetic acid to a beaker.
Add NaOH solution dropwise from a burette.
Monitor pH continuously — rises gradually (buffer action).
At half-equivalence point: pH = pKa = 4.76 (buffer).
At equivalence point: pH ≈ 8.7 (slightly alkaline).
Use phenolphthalein indicator — turns pink at equivalence.
Record the titration curve — note the buffer region.
CH3COOH+NaOH.observation: Gentle neutralization. Sodium acetate solution formed. pH > 7 at equivalence.

NH4OH+NaOH.title: 🧪 NaOH + NH₄OH — Highly Alkaline Mix
NH4OH+NaOH.formula: NaOH + NH₄OH → Na⁺ + OH⁻ + NH₄⁺ + OH⁻  (highly alkaline, NH₃ released)
NH4OH+NaOH.sub: Base mixture | pH > 13 | Ammonia fumes intensify | No net reaction
NH4OH+NaOH.overview:
Mixing NaOH and NH₄OH gives a highly alkaline solution.
NaOH suppresses NH₄⁺ equilibrium, releasing more NH₃. Ammonia fumes intensify.
NH4OH+NaOH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work entirely inside the fume hood.
Measure NaOH solution into a beaker.
Add NH₄OH slowly while stirring.
Observe: pungent ammonia smell intensifies.
Measure pH — very high (pH > 13).
NaOH suppresses NH₄⁺ equilibrium, releasing more NH₃.
Dispose carefully — neutralize with dilute HCl.
NH4OH+NaOH.observation: Highly alkaline solution. pH > 13. Strong ammonia odor released.

CaCO3+NaOH.title: 🧪 CaCO₃ + NaOH — No Significant Reaction
CaCO3+NaOH.formula: CaCO₃ + 2NaOH → No reaction  (CaCO₃ is insoluble and unreactive with bases)
CaCO3+NaOH.sub: No reaction | CaCO₃ insoluble in base | Both are alkaline | Mixture only
CaCO3+NaOH.overview:
CaCO₃ does not react significantly with NaOH under normal lab conditions.
CaCO₃ is insoluble in alkaline solution. Both are basic — no acid-base reaction occurs.
steps: Add NaOH solution to a beaker.
Add CaCO₃ powder and stir.
Observe: no visible reaction — no gas, no precipitate dissolving.
CaCO₃ remains insoluble in alkaline solution.
Measure pH — alkaline (due to NaOH).
No chemical change has occurred.
Filter off undissolved CaCO₃.
CaCO3+NaOH.observation: No reaction. CaCO₃ remains undissolved. Alkaline solution due to NaOH.

Mg+NaOH.title: ⚗ Mg + NaOH — Very Slow Reaction
Mg+NaOH.sub: Very slow | Mg reacts slowly with concentrated NaOH | H₂ evolved slowly
Mg+NaOH.overview:
Mg reacts very slowly with concentrated NaOH solution, releasing H₂ gas.
Unlike Al, Mg does not react readily with dilute alkalis at room temperature.
Mg+NaOH.tools: • Test Tube  • Concentrated NaOH  • Lighted Splint
steps: Place Mg ribbon in a test tube.
Add concentrated NaOH solution (not dilute).
Observe: very slow or no visible reaction at room temperature.
Heat gently — very slow H₂ evolution may occur.
Test any evolved gas with a lighted splint — faint squeaky pop.
Note: Mg reacts much more slowly with alkali than with acid.
Record rate comparison with Mg + HCl.
Mg+NaOH.observation: Very slow or no visible reaction. Mg is much less reactive with alkalis than acids.

C2H5OH+NaOH.title: 🧪 Ethanol + NaOH — No Significant Reaction
C2H5OH+NaOH.formula: C₂H₅OH + NaOH → No significant reaction  (alcohols are too weakly acidic)
C2H5OH+NaOH.sub: No net reaction | Alcohols do not react with dilute NaOH | pKa ~16
C2H5OH+NaOH.overview:
Ethanol does not react with dilute NaOH under normal lab conditions.
Alcohols have pKa ~16 — too weak to react with NaOH. No visible change occurs.
steps: Add NaOH solution to a beaker.
Add ethanol and stir.
No visible reaction — solutions mix freely.
No gas, no precipitate, no color change.
Alcohols are too weakly acidic (pKa ~16) to react with NaOH.
Measure pH — alkaline (due to NaOH remaining).
This is an important contrast to carboxylic acids, which do react.
C2H5OH+NaOH.observation: No reaction. Ethanol and NaOH mix without reacting.

CH3COOH+NaHCO3.title: 🫧 Vinegar + Baking Soda
CH3COOH+NaHCO3.sub: Weak Acid + Carbonate | Gentle CO₂ fizz | Sodium acetate | Classic kitchen reaction
CH3COOH+NaHCO3.overview:
CH₃COOH reacts with NaHCO₃ releasing CO₂ and forming sodium acetate.
Gentler than HCl reaction — classic kitchen chemistry (vinegar + baking soda).
steps: Pour 10 mL of acetic acid into a beaker.
Add a spatula of NaHCO₃ powder slowly.
Observe: gentle fizzing — CO₂ released.
Pass gas through limewater — turns milky (confirms CO₂).
The vinegar smell decreases as acid is consumed.
Final solution: sodium acetate (CH₃COONa) — slightly alkaline.
This is exactly the 'vinegar + baking soda' kitchen reaction.
CH3COOH+NaHCO3.observation: Gentle CO₂ fizz. Vinegar smell reduces. Sodium acetate solution formed.

NH4OH+NaHCO3.title: 🧪 NH₄OH + NaHCO₃ — Alkaline Mixture
NH4OH+NaHCO3.formula: NH₄OH + NaHCO₃ → NH₄HCO₃ + NaOH  (partial) OR mixed alkaline solution
NH4OH+NaHCO3.sub: Two bases | Mildly alkaline mixture | No dramatic reaction | pH ~9
NH4OH+NaHCO3.overview:
NH₄OH and NaHCO₃ are both weakly alkaline.
They coexist without significant reaction. The solution is mildly alkaline.
NH4OH+NaHCO3.tools: • Beaker  • Stirrer  • pH Meter  • Fume Hood
NH4OH+NaHCO3.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work near fume hood — NH₄OH releases ammonia.
Add NaHCO₃ solution to a beaker.
Add NH₄OH slowly and stir.
No vigorous reaction — mild ammonia odor.
Measure pH — mildly alkaline (pH 8–9).
No precipitate, no gas evolved.
This mixture is used in some analytical procedures.
NH4OH+NaHCO3.observation: Mild alkaline mixture. pH 8–9. Faint ammonia odor. No vigorous reaction.

CaCO3+NaHCO3.title: 🧪 CaCO₃ + NaHCO₃ — No Reaction
CaCO3+NaHCO3.formula: CaCO₃ + NaHCO₃ → No significant reaction  (both are carbonates/basic salts)
CaCO3+NaHCO3.sub: No reaction | Both are carbonates | Neutral to slightly alkaline mixture
CaCO3+NaHCO3.overview:
CaCO₃ and NaHCO₃ do not react with each other.
Both are carbonates/salts. The mixture is neutral to slightly alkaline.
steps: Mix CaCO₃ powder with NaHCO₃ solution.
Stir thoroughly.
No gas, no precipitate, no color change.
Measure pH — slightly alkaline.
Both are stable carbonates — no reaction occurs.
This confirms that two bases/salts don't react with each other.
Filter off undissolved CaCO₃.
CaCO3+NaHCO3.observation: No reaction. Both are alkaline — no acid-base reaction. Slightly alkaline mixture.

Mg+NaHCO3.title: 🧪 Mg + NaHCO₃ — Very Slow / No Reaction
Mg+NaHCO3.formula: Mg + NaHCO₃ → No significant reaction at room temperature
Mg+NaHCO3.sub: No visible reaction | Mg does not react with weakly alkaline bicarbonate
Mg+NaHCO3.overview:
Magnesium does not react with sodium bicarbonate solution under normal conditions.
NaHCO₃ is too weakly acidic/basic to attack Mg metal at room temperature.
steps: Place Mg ribbon in a beaker.
Add NaHCO₃ solution.
Observe: no visible reaction.
No gas, no color change, no precipitate.
NaHCO₃ is too weakly alkaline to react with Mg.
Compare with Mg + HCl — dramatic difference.
Mg remains unchanged — remove and dry.
Mg+NaHCO3.observation: No visible reaction. Mg remains unchanged in NaHCO₃ solution.

C2H5OH+NaHCO3.title: 🧪 Ethanol + NaHCO₃ — No Reaction
C2H5OH+NaHCO3.formula: C₂H₅OH + NaHCO₃ → No reaction  (alcohols too weakly acidic for NaHCO₃)
C2H5OH+NaHCO3.sub: No reaction | Alcohols (pKa ~16) too weak to react with NaHCO₃ | Carboxylic acids do
C2H5OH+NaHCO3.overview:
Ethanol does not react with NaHCO₃.
This is a key test: carboxylic acids fizz with NaHCO₃, but alcohols do not.
steps: Add NaHCO₃ solution to a test tube.
Add a few drops of ethanol.
Observe: no fizzing — no CO₂ evolved.
No visible reaction occurs.
This distinguishes alcohols from carboxylic acids.
Compare with CH₃COOH + NaHCO₃ — that does fizz.
Important diagnostic test in organic chemistry.
C2H5OH+NaHCO3.observation: No reaction. No CO₂ evolved. Distinguishes alcohols from carboxylic acids.

CH3COOH+NH4OH.title: ⚗ NH₄OH + CH₃COOH — see CH3COOH+NH4OH
CH3COOH+NH4OH.sub: Weak Acid–Weak Base | Ammonium acetate | pH ≈ 7
CH3COOH+NH4OH.overview:
CH₃COOH + NH₄OH → CH₃COONH₄ + H₂O
Ammonium acetate (nearly neutral salt) formed.
CH3COOH+NH4OH.tools: • Beaker  • Dropper  • pH Meter  • Fume Hood
CH3COOH+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work near fume hood — NH₄OH releases ammonia.
Measure 10 mL of acetic acid.
Add NH₄OH slowly while stirring.
Ammonia odor decreases as reaction proceeds.
At equivalence: ammonium acetate formed.
Measure pH — close to 7.
Ammonium acetate used as buffer and food additive.
CH3COOH+NH4OH.observation: Ammonium acetate solution formed. pH ≈ 7. Ammonia smell decreases.

CH3COOH+CaCO3.title: 🫧 Marble Chips + Acetic Acid
CH3COOH+CaCO3.sub: Acid + Carbonate | Gentle CO₂ | Slower than HCl | Rate comparison
CH3COOH+CaCO3.overview:
CaCO₃ reacts with acetic acid (weak acid) much more slowly than with HCl.
Calcium acetate solution forms. Classic rate-of-reaction comparison experiment.
steps: Place marble chips in a beaker.
Add 5% acetic acid solution.
Observe: gentle, slow fizzing — much slower than HCl.
Pass gas through limewater — confirms CO₂.
Calcium acetate remains in solution.
Compare rate with HCl reaction — weak acid is slower.
This demonstrates acid strength affects reaction rate.
CH3COOH+CaCO3.observation: Gentle CO₂ fizz. Slower than HCl. Calcium acetate in solution.

CH3COOH+Mg.title: ⚡ Magnesium + Acetic Acid
CH3COOH+Mg.sub: Metal + Weak Acid | Slower H₂ than with HCl | Magnesium acetate formed
CH3COOH+Mg.overview:
Mg reacts with acetic acid (weak acid) more slowly than with HCl.
H₂ gas and magnesium acetate are produced. Rate depends on acid concentration.
steps: Place a strip of magnesium ribbon in a test tube.
Add 5% acetic acid solution.
Observe: slower and gentler fizzing vs HCl (weak acid).
H₂ gas is still confirmed by squeaky pop test.
Vinegar smell reduces as acetic acid is consumed.
Magnesium acetate solution remains — colorless.
Compare rate with HCl reaction — weaker acid = slower rate.
CH3COOH+Mg.observation: Gentle H₂ fizz (slower than HCl). Magnesium acetate forms. Squeaky pop.

C2H5OH+CH3COOH.title: 🧴 Esterification — Ethyl Acetate
C2H5OH+CH3COOH.sub: Esterification | Fruity ethyl acetate aroma | Reversible | H₂SO₄ catalyst needed
C2H5OH+CH3COOH.overview:
C₂H₅OH + CH₃COOH ⇌ Ethyl acetate + H₂O (reversible equilibrium).
Fruity-smelling ethyl acetate ester formed. Requires H₂SO₄ catalyst and heat.
C2H5OH+CH3COOH.tools: • Round Flask  • Reflux Condenser  • Burner  • H₂SO₄ catalyst  • Fume Hood
C2H5OH+CH3COOH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Mix ethanol and acetic acid in a round-bottom flask.
Add a few drops of H₂SO₄ as catalyst.
Heat under reflux for 15 minutes.
Observe sweet fruity smell (ethyl acetate).
Separate ester layer on cooling.
Wash with NaHCO₃ solution to remove acid.
Collect and identify ethyl acetate by its fruity smell.
C2H5OH+CH3COOH.observation: Sweet fruity smell. Oily ethyl acetate ester layer separates on standing.

CaCO3+NH4OH.title: 🧪 CaCO₃ + NH₄OH — No Reaction
CaCO3+NH4OH.formula: CaCO₃ + NH₄OH → No reaction  (both alkaline, CaCO₃ insoluble in weak base)
CaCO3+NH4OH.sub: No reaction | Both are basic | CaCO₃ insoluble | Alkaline mixture
CaCO3+NH4OH.overview:
CaCO₃ and NH₄OH do not react with each other.
Both are alkaline. CaCO₃ is insoluble in weak alkaline solution.
CaCO3+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work near fume hood — NH₄OH releases ammonia.
Add NH₄OH solution to a beaker.
Add CaCO₃ powder and stir.
No vigorous reaction — mild ammonia odor.
CaCO₃ remains undissolved.
Measure pH — mildly alkaline.
Filter off undissolved CaCO₃.
CaCO3+NH4OH.observation: No reaction. CaCO₃ remains insoluble. Mildly alkaline mixture.

Mg+NH4OH.title: 🧪 Mg + NH₄OH — Very Slow Reaction
Mg+NH4OH.formula: Mg + 2NH₄OH → Mg(OH)₂↓ + 2NH₃↑ + H₂↑  (very slow, requires concentrated)
Mg+NH4OH.sub: Very slow | Mg dissolves slowly in concentrated NH₄OH | H₂ and NH₃ evolved
Mg+NH4OH.overview:
Mg reacts very slowly with NH₄OH solution, releasing H₂ and ammonia.
Mg(OH)₂ white precipitate may form. Much slower than with mineral acids.
Mg+NH4OH.tools: • Test Tube  • Fume Hood  • Lighted Splint
Mg+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work in fume hood — NH₄OH releases ammonia.
Place Mg ribbon in a test tube.
Add NH₄OH solution (concentrated if possible).
Observe: very slow bubbling — H₂ evolved very slowly.
Pungent ammonia odor present throughout.
White Mg(OH)₂ precipitate may form.
Compare rate with Mg + HCl — much slower.
Mg+NH4OH.observation: Very slow H₂ bubbling. Ammonia odor. Much slower than with mineral acids.

C2H5OH+NH4OH.title: 🧪 Ethanol + NH₄OH — No Reaction
C2H5OH+NH4OH.sub: No reaction | Both neutral/weakly alkaline | Miscible liquids | No chemical change
C2H5OH+NH4OH.overview:
Ethanol and NH₄OH do not react with each other.
Both are neutral to weakly alkaline. They mix freely as miscible liquids.
C2H5OH+NH4OH.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work near fume hood — NH₄OH releases ammonia.
Mix ethanol and NH₄OH in a test tube.
No visible reaction — solutions mix freely.
No gas, no precipitate, no color change.
Pungent ammonia odor present from NH₄OH.
Measure pH — mildly alkaline (due to NH₄OH).
No chemical reaction has occurred.
C2H5OH+NH4OH.observation: No reaction. Miscible mixture. Mild ammonia odor from NH₄OH.

CaCO3+Mg.title: 🧪 CaCO₃ + Mg — No Reaction at Room Temperature
CaCO3+Mg.formula: CaCO₃ + Mg → No reaction at room temperature  (requires very high temperature)
CaCO3+Mg.sub: No reaction at room temp | At very high temp Mg can reduce CaCO₃ | Solid-solid
CaCO3+Mg.overview:
CaCO₃ and Mg do not react at room temperature.
At very high temperatures, Mg can reduce CaCO₃, but this is not a standard lab reaction.
steps: Place Mg ribbon and CaCO₃ powder together.
Add water to create a slurry.
Observe: no visible reaction.
No gas, no color change, no heat produced.
Both remain unchanged at room temperature.
Very high temperature (thermite conditions) needed for reaction.
Record: no observable change.
CaCO3+Mg.observation: No reaction at room temperature. Both remain unchanged.

C2H5OH+Mg.title: ⚗ Ethanol + Mg — Slow Reaction (Sodium Ethoxide Analogy)
C2H5OH+Mg.formula: 2C₂H₅OH + Mg → Mg(OC₂H₅)₂ + H₂↑  (very slow at room temperature)
C2H5OH+Mg.sub: Very slow | Mg reacts with alcohol to form magnesium ethoxide | H₂ evolved slowly
C2H5OH+Mg.overview:
Mg reacts very slowly with ethanol to form magnesium ethoxide and H₂.
Much slower than Mg with water. Requires fresh Mg surface (remove oxide layer).
C2H5OH+Mg.tools: • Test Tube  • Lighted Splint  • Fume Hood
C2H5OH+Mg.safety: • Goggles  • Gloves  • Lab Coat  • Fume Hood
steps: Work in fume hood — ethanol is flammable.
Clean Mg ribbon surface with sandpaper to remove oxide layer.
Place Mg in a test tube.
Add dry absolute ethanol.
Observe: very slow bubbling — H₂ evolved very slowly.
Warm gently to accelerate reaction.
Magnesium ethoxide (white solid) may form on evaporation.
C2H5OH+Mg.observation: Very slow H₂ evolution. Magnesium ethoxide forms on complete reaction.

C2H5OH+CaCO3.title: 🧪 CaCO₃ + Ethanol — No Reaction
C2H5OH+CaCO3.sub: No reaction | CaCO₃ insoluble in ethanol | No acid-base or redox reaction
C2H5OH+CaCO3.overview:
CaCO₃ does not react with ethanol.
CaCO₃ is insoluble in organic solvents. No chemical reaction occurs.
steps: Add CaCO₃ powder to a test tube.
Add ethanol and stir.
Observe: CaCO₃ remains undissolved.
No gas, no color change, no heat.
CaCO₃ is insoluble in organic solvents.
Settle and observe: white powder at bottom.
No chemical reaction has occurred.
C2H5OH+CaCO3.observation: No reaction. CaCO₃ remains insoluble in ethanol.

DEFAULT.title: ⚗ Unknown Combination
DEFAULT.sub: Select a recognized chemical combination to see the reaction
DEFAULT.overview:
This chemical combination is not yet in the database.
Try a different set of chemicals, or add this reaction to the dataset.
steps: This combination is not in the experiment database.
Consult a chemistry reference for this reaction.
Ensure all safety equipment is worn regardless.
Proceed only under qualified supervision.
To add this reaction: see the _meta.howToAddReaction field.
DEFAULT.observation: Unknown reaction outcome for this combination.
